title: Errors, Files & Text
description: Exceptions, file handling, context managers, and regular expressions.

topics.title: Exceptions
topics.intro: Exceptions are Python's way of signalling and recovering from runtime errors without crashing the whole program.
summary: When something goes wrong at runtime — a missing file, bad user input, a network timeout, dividing by zero — Python raises an exception. If nothing handles it, the exception propagates up the call stack, prints a traceback, and the program exits. The `try`/`except` statement lets you intercept that exception and decide what to do instead: retry, fall back to a default, log it, or re-raise it with more context. Putting only the risky line inside `try` and catching the *specific* exception type (`FileNotFoundError`, `ValueError`, `KeyError`) — never a bare `except:` — keeps you from accidentally swallowing bugs like typos and `KeyboardInterrupt`.

A full handler has four clauses. `try` holds the code that might fail; one or more `except` clauses match exception types (you can bind the instance with `as e` to inspect it); the optional `else` runs only when no exception was raised, which is the clean place to put the "success" code so it is not accidentally guarded by the same `try`; and `finally` always runs, exception or not, making it the right place for cleanup that must happen no matter what. You raise your own errors with `raise ValueError("message")`, and you preserve the original cause when wrapping an error using `raise NewError(...) from err`, which produces a readable chained traceback.

Modern Python (3.11+) adds two features worth knowing. Exception groups (`ExceptionGroup`) bundle multiple unrelated errors raised together — common in concurrent code — and the `except*` syntax handles them selectively. And `exc.add_note("extra context")` attaches human-readable notes to an exception that show up in the traceback, which is handy for debugging. Define custom exceptions by subclassing `Exception` (not `BaseException`) so callers can catch your domain errors precisely, and prefer raising a specific built-in when one already fits.
keyPoints: Catch specific exception types (`ValueError`, `FileNotFoundError`), never a bare `except:` — it hides bugs and swallows Ctrl-C.
`try`/`except`/`else`/`finally`: `else` runs on success only, `finally` always runs (use it for cleanup).
Bind the exception with `except SomeError as e:` to inspect its message, args, or attributes.
Raise errors with `raise ValueError(...)`; preserve the cause when re-wrapping with `raise NewError(...) from err`.
Subclass `Exception` (not `BaseException`) for custom domain errors so callers can catch them precisely.
Python 3.11+ adds `ExceptionGroup`/`except*` for multiple errors and `exc.add_note()` for richer tracebacks.

code.title: try / except / else / finally
code.code:
def read_int(text):
    try:
        value = int(text)          # may raise ValueError
    except ValueError as e:
        print(f"not a number: {e}")
        return None
    else:
        # runs only if no exception was raised
        print(f"parsed {value!r}")
        return value
    finally:
        # always runs, success or failure
        print("done parsing")

read_int("42")    # parsed 42 / done parsing
read_int("oops")  # not a number / done parsing

code.title: Custom exceptions and chaining
Catching multiple types and adding notes
code.code:
def fetch(item, db):
    try:
        return db[item]
    except (KeyError, TypeError) as e:   # one handler, several types
        e.add_note(f"while fetching {item!r}")  # 3.11+: richer traceback
        raise

fetch("missing", {"a": 1})  # KeyError: 'missing'  + note in traceback

handsOn.goal: Build a small resilient parser that validates input, raises a custom exception with chaining, and always cleans up — exercising every clause of try/except/else/finally.

steps.title: Catch the specific error, not everything
steps.explanation: Wrap only the risky call in `try` and catch the exact exception type. Returning a sensible default instead of crashing is the whole point of exception handling.

steps.title: Use else and finally correctly
steps.explanation: `else` runs only when the `try` block succeeded, so success logic lives there and is not accidentally protected by the same handler. `finally` runs no matter what — the right home for cleanup.

steps.title: Define a custom exception and chain the cause
steps.explanation: Subclass `Exception` for a domain-specific error, then wrap the low-level error with `raise ... from err` so the original cause stays visible in the traceback.

steps.title: Re-raise with an added note
steps.explanation: Sometimes you want to catch, attach context, and let the error keep propagating. A bare `raise` re-raises the current exception, and `add_note()` (3.11+) enriches the traceback without changing the type.

practice: Make `to_float` accept a `default` argument and return it (instead of `None`) when parsing fails.
Add an `except OverflowError` branch to `divide` and trigger it with `divide(1e308, 1e-308)`.
Raise `TemperatureError` from `parse_celsius` for values above 1000 with a clear message, then catch and print it.
Wrap a `json.loads(...)` call so a `json.JSONDecodeError` is re-raised as a custom `ConfigError` using `from`.
Write a `retry(fn, attempts=3)` helper that calls `fn()`, catches exceptions, retries up to `attempts` times, and re-raises the last error if all attempts fail.

topics.title: File Handling
topics.intro: Reading and writing files is how programs persist data — and `with open(...)` plus `pathlib` make it safe and idiomatic in modern Python.
summary: You read and write files in Python with the built-in `open(path, mode, encoding=...)`, which returns a file object. The mode string controls intent: `"r"` read (default), `"w"` write (truncates), `"a"` append, `"x"` exclusive create (fails if the file exists), and a trailing `"b"` for binary (`"rb"`, `"wb"`) versus text mode. Always pass `encoding="utf-8"` for text files — relying on the platform default is a classic source of cross-machine bugs. The cardinal rule is to use `open` as a context manager: `with open(...) as f:` guarantees the file is closed even if an exception is raised inside the block, so you never leak file handles.

Once open, a text file is iterable line by line — `for line in f:` streams one line at a time and is memory-efficient for huge files, whereas `f.read()` slurps the whole file into a string and `f.readlines()` returns a list of lines. To write, use `f.write(text)` (no implicit newline) or `f.writelines(list_of_strings)`. For structured data, the standard library's `json` module pairs `json.dump(obj, f)` / `json.load(f)` to work directly with file objects and `json.dumps` / `json.loads` for strings; pass `indent=2` to pretty-print and remember `json.load` raises `json.JSONDecodeError` on malformed input, which you should handle.

Modern Python favors `pathlib.Path` over string paths and the older `os.path` functions. A `Path` object joins segments with the `/` operator (`Path("data") / "in.txt"`), and offers convenience methods like `path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")`, `path.write_text(...)`, `path.exists()`, `path.glob(...)`, and `path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)`. For the common case of reading or writing an entire small file, `Path.read_text` / `write_text` are one-liners that open, transfer, and close for you. Reach for `with open(...)` when you need streaming, append mode, or fine control over the file object.
keyPoints: Always use `with open(...) as f:` — the context manager closes the file automatically, even on error.
Pass `encoding="utf-8"` explicitly for text files; never depend on the platform's default encoding.
Modes: `r` read, `w` write (truncates!), `a` append, `x` exclusive create; add `b` for binary.
Iterate `for line in f:` to stream large files; `f.read()` loads everything into memory at once.
Use `json.dump`/`json.load` for file objects and `json.dumps`/`json.loads` for strings; `indent=2` pretty-prints.
Prefer `pathlib.Path` (`/` join, `read_text`, `write_text`, `exists`, `mkdir`) over `os.path` string juggling.

code.title: Read and write text safely
code.code:
# write (truncates the file if it exists)
with open("notes.txt", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write("first line\n")
    f.writelines(["second\n", "third\n"])

# read line by line (memory-efficient for big files)
with open("notes.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    for n, line in enumerate(f, start=1):
        print(n, line.rstrip())

# append a line without erasing the file
with open("notes.txt", "a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write("appended\n")

code.title: Read and write JSON
Modern paths with pathlib
code.code:
from pathlib import Path

data_dir = Path("data")
data_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)  # like mkdir -p

out = data_dir / "hello.txt"   # join with /
out.write_text("hi there\n", encoding="utf-8")  # open+write+close in one call

print(out.exists())            # True
print(out.read_text(encoding="utf-8").strip())  # hi there
print(out.suffix, out.stem, out.name)  # .txt hello hello.txt

resources.title: json — JSON encoder and decoder (official docs)
Working With Files in Python (Real Python)
Working With JSON Data in Python (Real Python)

handsOn.goal: Build a tiny persistent note-taker that appends lines to a text file and stores structured records as JSON, using `with open`, the `json` module, and `pathlib`.

steps.title: Write and read a text file with the context manager
steps.explanation: `with open(...)` opens the file, runs the block, and closes the handle automatically. Writing in `"w"` mode truncates; reading streams the file line by line.
steps.python:
# notes.py
LINES = ["buy milk", "call Ada", "ship release"]

with open("todo.txt", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    for line in LINES:
        f.write(line + "\n")

with open("todo.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    for i, line in enumerate(f, start=1):
        print(f"{i}. {line.rstrip()}")

steps.title: Append without overwriting
steps.explanation: Mode `"a"` opens for appending, so existing content survives and new writes go to the end. This is how logs and growing lists are kept.
steps.python:
def add_todo(item):
    with open("todo.txt", "a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        f.write(item + "\n")

add_todo("water plants")
add_todo("read docs")

with open("todo.txt", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    print(f.read())

steps.title: Persist structured records as JSON
steps.explanation: Text files are fine for lines, but structured data wants JSON. `json.dump` writes a Python object to a file; `json.load` reads it back into Python types.
steps.python:
import json

notes = [
    {"id": 1, "text": "buy milk", "done": False},
    {"id": 2, "text": "call Ada", "done": True},
]

with open("notes.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    json.dump(notes, f, indent=2)

with open("notes.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    loaded = json.load(f)

done = [n["text"] for n in loaded if n["done"]]
print("completed:", done)

steps.title: Switch to pathlib one-liners
steps.explanation: For whole-file reads and writes, `pathlib.Path` removes the boilerplate: `write_text`/`read_text` open, transfer, and close in a single call, and `/` joins path segments cleanly.
steps.python:
from pathlib import Path
import json

path = Path("data") / "notes.json"
path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

path.write_text(json.dumps(notes, indent=2), encoding="utf-8")
restored = json.loads(path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
print(len(restored), "notes loaded from", path)

practice: Add a `count_lines(path)` function that opens a file and returns the number of non-empty lines.
Make `add_todo` create the file with mode `"x"` the first time and fall back to `"a"` if it already exists (catch `FileExistsError`).
Add a `mark_done(notes, note_id)` function that flips `done` to True for a matching id, then save the list back to `notes.json`.
Read `notes.json`, but wrap `json.load` in a try/except so a corrupted file prints a friendly message instead of crashing.
Use `Path("data").glob("*.json")` to list every JSON file you have written and print each filename with its size in bytes (`p.stat().st_size`).

topics.title: Context Managers
topics.intro: Context managers automate setup-and-teardown around a block of code, so resources like files, locks, and connections are always released.
summary: A context manager is any object usable with the `with` statement. When you write `with cm as value:`, Python calls the object's `__enter__()` method on the way in (its return value is bound to `value`) and guarantees a call to `__exit__()` on the way out — whether the block finishes normally, returns early, or raises an exception. That guarantee is the whole point: it replaces fragile `try`/`finally` cleanup with a single readable line and makes resource leaks (unclosed files, held locks, open sockets) far harder to write. `open()`, `threading.Lock`, and database connections are all context managers for exactly this reason.

You can write your own in two ways. The class form implements `__enter__(self)` and `__exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback)`; returning a truthy value from `__exit__` suppresses an exception, while returning falsy (the default) lets it propagate. The far more common form uses the `@contextmanager` decorator from `contextlib`: write a generator that does setup, `yield`s the resource, and does cleanup after the `yield` — typically inside a `try`/`finally` so cleanup runs even on error. The code before `yield` is the `__enter__`; the code after is the `__exit__`.

The `contextlib` module ships several ready-made helpers worth reaching for: `suppress(Exception)` swallows specific exceptions in a block (cleaner than an empty `except`), `closing(thing)` calls `.close()` on exit for objects that lack context-manager support, `redirect_stdout`/`redirect_stderr` temporarily reroute output, `nullcontext()` is a do-nothing stand-in when a context manager is optional, and `ExitStack` manages a dynamic number of context managers (and async variants exist via `AsyncExitStack`). You can also enter several managers in one statement: `with open(a) as f, open(b) as g:`.
keyPoints: `with cm as x:` guarantees `__exit__` (cleanup) runs even if the block raises — no leaked files, locks, or connections.
Class form implements `__enter__`/`__exit__`; `__exit__` returning truthy suppresses the exception, falsy re-raises it.
The `@contextlib.contextmanager` decorator turns a generator into a context manager: setup, `yield`, then cleanup in `finally`.
Open several at once: `with open(a) as f, open(b) as g:` — each is entered and exited correctly.
`contextlib` helpers: `suppress`, `closing`, `redirect_stdout`, `nullcontext`, and `ExitStack` for a dynamic number of managers.
Put cleanup after `yield` inside a `try`/`finally` so it still runs when the body raises.

code.title: Generator-based context manager
code.code:
import time
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def timer(label):
    start = time.perf_counter()   # setup == __enter__
    try:
        yield                     # the value bound by `as`
    finally:
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start  # cleanup == __exit__
        print(f"{label}: {elapsed:.4f}s")

with timer("work"):
    total = sum(i * i for i in range(1_000_000))
# prints: work: 0.0XXXs  (even if the block had raised)

code.title: Class-based context manager
code.code:
class Resource:
    def __enter__(self):
        print("acquire")
        return self                 # bound to `as r`

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, tb):
        print("release")
        # return True to swallow the exception; False/None re-raises it
        return False

with Resource() as r:
    print("using", r)
# acquire / using / release  (release runs even on exception)

code.title: Handy contextlib helpers
code.code:
from contextlib import suppress, ExitStack

# suppress: ignore a specific error in a block
with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
    open("maybe-missing.txt")  # no crash if absent

# ExitStack: manage a dynamic number of files
filenames = ["a.txt", "b.txt", "c.txt"]
with ExitStack() as stack:
    files = [stack.enter_context(open(n, "w", encoding="utf-8"))
             for n in filenames]
    for f in files:
        f.write("hello\n")
# every file is closed here, in reverse order

resources.title: contextlib — utilities for with-statement contexts (official docs)

handsOn.goal: Write your own context managers both ways — as a class and with the `@contextmanager` decorator — and use `contextlib` helpers to manage cleanup automatically.

steps.title: Build a class-based context manager
steps.explanation: Implement `__enter__` (setup, returns the value bound by `as`) and `__exit__` (cleanup, runs no matter what). Watch that `__exit__` fires even when the block raises.

steps.title: Do the same with @contextmanager
steps.explanation: The decorator form is shorter: everything before `yield` is setup, everything after is cleanup. Wrapping the `yield` in `try`/`finally` guarantees cleanup even on error.

steps.title: Prove cleanup runs on exceptions
steps.explanation: Raise an error inside the block. Because cleanup lives in `finally` (decorator) or `__exit__` (class), it still runs before the exception propagates out.

steps.title: Use suppress and ExitStack
steps.explanation: `suppress` replaces an empty `except` block, and `ExitStack` enters a variable number of context managers, closing them all in reverse on exit.
steps.python:
from contextlib import suppress, ExitStack

with suppress(ZeroDivisionError):
    print(1 / 0)        # silently ignored
print("kept going")

names = ["x.txt", "y.txt"]
with ExitStack() as stack:
    files = [stack.enter_context(open(n, "w", encoding="utf-8"))
             for n in names]
    for f in files:
        f.write("data\n")
print("all files closed")

practice: Make the class-based `Section` print how long the block took by recording `time.perf_counter()` in `__enter__` and `__exit__`.
Write a `@contextmanager` called `cd(path)` that changes the working directory on enter and restores the original on exit (`os.getcwd` / `os.chdir`).
Change `Section.__exit__` to return `True` when the exception type is `ValueError`, and confirm a `ValueError` raised inside is swallowed.
Use `contextlib.redirect_stdout` to capture `print` output into an `io.StringIO` buffer and then print the captured text.
Write a context manager that opens two files (input and output) with a single `with` statement and copies lines from one to the other.

topics.title: Glob
topics.intro: The `glob` module finds files by Unix shell-style wildcard patterns — the quickest way to list "all the `.csv` files" without manual directory walking.
summary: `glob` matches pathnames against shell wildcard patterns instead of regular expressions, which makes it perfect for file discovery. The three wildcards are `*` (any run of characters except the path separator), `?` (exactly one character), and `[...]` (a character set, e.g. `[0-9]`). `glob.glob("*.txt")` returns a list of matching paths in arbitrary order; `glob.iglob(...)` returns an iterator for large result sets; and `glob.escape(name)` escapes special characters so you can match a literal filename that contains `*`, `?`, or brackets. Note that glob ignores dotfiles (names starting with `.`) by default, mirroring the shell.

For recursive search, pass `recursive=True` and use the `**` pattern, which matches any number of nested directories: `glob.glob("**/*.py", recursive=True)` finds every `.py` file in the tree below the current directory. The `root_dir` parameter (Python 3.10+) lets you search relative to a directory without `chdir`, and `include_hidden=True` (3.11+) includes dotfiles. Because results come back unsorted, wrap the call in `sorted(...)` whenever you need deterministic output.

In modern code, `pathlib.Path.glob()` and `Path.rglob()` are the object-oriented equivalents and are usually preferred: `Path("src").rglob("*.py")` yields `Path` objects you can immediately call `.read_text()`, `.stat()`, or `.name` on, no string juggling required. Reach for the `glob` module when you want plain string paths or are matching against a single ad-hoc pattern; reach for `pathlib` when you are already working with `Path` objects, which is the norm in new projects.
keyPoints: Wildcards: `*` (any characters, not `/`), `?` (one character), `[seq]` (a character set) — shell-style, not regex.
`glob.glob(pattern)` returns a list; `glob.iglob(pattern)` returns an iterator; results are unsorted, so wrap in `sorted()`.
Use `recursive=True` with the `**` pattern to descend into subdirectories (`**/*.py`).
Dotfiles are skipped by default; pass `include_hidden=True` (3.11+) to include them; `glob.escape()` matches literal wildcards.
`root_dir=` (3.10+) searches relative to a directory without changing the working directory.
Prefer `pathlib.Path.glob()` / `Path.rglob()` in new code — they yield `Path` objects ready for `.read_text()`, `.stat()`, etc.

code.title: Basic and recursive globbing
code.code:
import glob

# all .txt files in the current directory (unsorted)
print(sorted(glob.glob("*.txt")))

# one-character wildcard and a digit set
print(glob.glob("log?.txt"))      # log1.txt, logA.txt ...
print(glob.glob("data[0-9].csv")) # data0.csv ... data9.csv

# recurse into all subdirectories with **
for path in sorted(glob.glob("**/*.py", recursive=True)):
    print(path)

code.title: iglob, escape, and root_dir
code.code:
import glob

# iglob: lazy iterator, good for huge trees
for path in glob.iglob("**/*.log", recursive=True):
    print(path)

# match a filename that literally contains a wildcard
name = glob.escape("report[final].pdf")
print(glob.glob(name))

# search relative to a directory without chdir (3.10+)
print(glob.glob("*.json", root_dir="config"))

code.title: The pathlib equivalent (preferred in new code)
code.code:
from pathlib import Path

# rglob is recursive; glob is single-level
for p in sorted(Path("src").rglob("*.py")):
    print(p, p.stat().st_size, "bytes")

# Path objects chain straight into file operations
py_files = list(Path(".").glob("*.py"))
total_lines = sum(len(p.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines())
                  for p in py_files)
print("total lines:", total_lines)

resources.title: glob — Unix style pathname pattern expansion (official docs)

handsOn.goal: Create a small directory tree, then find files in it with the `glob` module and with `pathlib`, comparing single-level and recursive patterns.

steps.title: Build a tree of sample files
steps.explanation: Use `pathlib` to create nested directories and write a handful of files with different extensions so the glob patterns have something to match.
steps.python:
# setup_tree.py
from pathlib import Path

for sub in ["logs", "logs/2026", "data"]:
    Path(sub).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

files = ["a.txt", "b.txt", "data/x.csv", "data/y.csv",
         "logs/app.log", "logs/2026/app.log"]
for f in files:
    Path(f).write_text("sample\n", encoding="utf-8")
print("created", len(files), "files")

steps.title: Match a single level with the glob module
steps.explanation: `glob.glob` with a plain pattern only looks in the named directory (no recursion). Results come back unsorted, so wrap them in `sorted()` for stable output.
steps.python:
import glob

print("txt here:", sorted(glob.glob("*.txt")))      # a.txt, b.txt
print("csv in data:", sorted(glob.glob("data/*.csv")))
print("single char:", sorted(glob.glob("?.txt")))   # a.txt, b.txt

steps.title: Recurse with ** and recursive=True
steps.explanation: The `**` pattern only descends into subdirectories when `recursive=True` is passed. This finds the same-named log file at two different depths.
steps.python:
import glob

all_logs = sorted(glob.glob("**/*.log", recursive=True))
print("all logs:", all_logs)  # logs/app.log and logs/2026/app.log

all_files = sorted(glob.glob("**/*", recursive=True))
print("everything:", len(all_files), "paths")

steps.title: Do it the pathlib way
steps.explanation: `Path.glob` is single-level and `Path.rglob` recurses. Both yield `Path` objects you can immediately inspect — here we sum file sizes without any extra `open` calls.
steps.python:
from pathlib import Path

csvs = sorted(Path("data").glob("*.csv"))
print("csv paths:", [str(p) for p in csvs])

total = 0
for p in Path(".").rglob("*.log"):
    total += p.stat().st_size
print("total log bytes:", total)

practice: Use a character-set pattern like `data/[xy].csv` and confirm it matches both CSV files.
Count how many files have each extension by globbing `**/*` recursively and grouping on `Path(p).suffix`.
Add a hidden file `.secret.txt`, then show that `glob.glob('*.txt')` skips it but `glob.glob('*.txt', include_hidden=True)` finds it (3.11+).
Use `glob.iglob('**/*', recursive=True)` and stop after printing the first 3 results to see lazy iteration in action.
Rewrite the recursive log search as a one-liner with `Path('.').rglob('*.log')` and sort the results by file size.

topics.title: Regular Expressions
topics.intro: Regular expressions describe text patterns for searching, validating, and replacing — Python's `re` module is the standard tool for the job.
summary: A regular expression (regex) is a compact pattern language for matching text. Python exposes it through the `re` module. The core functions are `re.search(pattern, text)` (find the first match anywhere — usually what you want), `re.match(...)` (anchored at the start of the string), `re.fullmatch(...)` (the pattern must consume the whole string, ideal for validation), `re.findall(...)` (all non-overlapping matches as a list), `re.finditer(...)` (an iterator of richer `Match` objects), and `re.sub(pattern, repl, text)` (find-and-replace). Always write patterns as raw strings (`r"\d+"`) so backslashes reach the regex engine instead of being interpreted by Python first.

The building blocks: character classes `\d` (digit), `\w` (word char), `\s` (whitespace) and their uppercase negations; quantifiers `*` (zero or more), `+` (one or more), `?` (optional), and `{m,n}` (a range); anchors `^` / `$` (start/end) and `\b` (word boundary); `.` for any character; `|` for alternation; and `[...]` for custom sets. Parentheses create capturing groups you retrieve with `match.group(1)`, and named groups `(?P<name>...)` let you pull values out by name with `match.group("name")` — far more readable than numeric indices. Prefer `+?`/`*?` (lazy) over greedy quantifiers when matching delimited content like tags or quotes.

Two habits keep regex code maintainable. First, compile patterns you reuse with `pattern = re.compile(r"...")`, then call `pattern.search(text)`; it is faster in loops and reads better. Second, pass flags such as `re.IGNORECASE`, `re.MULTILINE` (so `^`/`$` match each line), and `re.VERBOSE` (which lets you space out a pattern and add `#` comments) to keep complex patterns legible. A `Match` object carries more than the text: `.group()`, `.groups()`, `.groupdict()`, and `.span()` give you the matched substrings and their positions. And know the limits — regex is the wrong tool for parsing nested structures like HTML or JSON; use a real parser there.
keyPoints: Use raw strings for patterns (`r"\d+"`) so backslashes reach the regex engine intact.
`search` finds the first match anywhere; `match` anchors at the start; `fullmatch` requires the whole string (great for validation).
`findall` returns a list of matches; `finditer` yields `Match` objects; `sub` does find-and-replace.
Capture with `(...)` and read via `group(1)`; prefer named groups `(?P<name>...)` and `group("name")` for readability.
`re.compile(...)` once for reused patterns; flags like `re.IGNORECASE`, `re.MULTILINE`, and `re.VERBOSE` keep patterns clean.
Regex cannot parse nested structures (HTML, JSON) — use a dedicated parser for those instead.

code.title: search, groups, and named groups
code.code:
import re

text = "Order 12345 shipped on 2026-06-29"

m = re.search(r"(?P<id>\d+).*?(?P<date>\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})", text)
if m:
    print(m.group("id"))     # 12345
    print(m.group("date"))   # 2026-06-29
    print(m.groupdict())     # {'id': '12345', 'date': '2026-06-29'}
    print(m.span("date"))    # (23, 33)

code.title: findall, finditer, and sub
code.code:
import re

log = "GET /a 200, POST /b 404, GET /c 500"

# every status code
print(re.findall(r"\b\d{3}\b", log))   # ['200', '404', '500']

# iterate with positions
for m in re.finditer(r"(GET|POST) (\S+)", log):
    print(m.group(1), "->", m.group(2))

# redact 3-digit codes
print(re.sub(r"\b\d{3}\b", "XXX", log))

code.title: compile, flags, and validation
code.code:
import re

# compile once, reuse many times; VERBOSE allows comments
EMAIL = re.compile(r"""
    ^[\w.+-]+      # local part
    @[\w-]+        # domain name
    (\.[\w-]+)+$   # one or more TLD segments
""", re.VERBOSE | re.IGNORECASE)

def is_email(s):
    return EMAIL.fullmatch(s) is not None

print(is_email("Ada@Example.COM"))  # True
print(is_email("nope@"))            # False

resources.title: Google for Education — Python Regular Expressions

handsOn.goal: Extract structured data from log lines, validate input with fullmatch, and clean text with sub — using compiled patterns and named groups.

steps.title: Search and capture with named groups
steps.explanation: `re.search` scans the whole string for the first match. Named groups `(?P<name>...)` make the captured pieces self-documenting when you read them back with `group("name")`.
steps.python:
# regex_lab.py
import re

line = '192.168.0.1 - - [29/Jun/2026] "GET /home" 200'
pattern = re.compile(
    r'(?P<ip>\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+).*"(?P<method>\w+) (?P<path>\S+)" (?P<status>\d+)'
)
m = pattern.search(line)
print(m.group("ip"))      # 192.168.0.1
print(m.group("method")) # GET
print(m.group("status")) # 200

steps.title: Find every match in a body of text
steps.explanation: `findall` returns just the matched strings; `finditer` returns `Match` objects so you also get groups and positions. Use them to pull all occurrences, not only the first.
steps.python:
import re

text = "prices: $12, $7, and $1999 today"
print(re.findall(r"\$(\d+)", text))   # ['12', '7', '1999']

for m in re.finditer(r"\$(\d+)", text):
    print(f"{m.group(0)} at {m.span()}")  # match text + (start, end)

steps.title: Validate input with fullmatch
steps.explanation: `fullmatch` succeeds only if the pattern consumes the entire string, which is exactly what validation needs. Compiling the pattern once keeps a reused validator fast and tidy.
steps.python:
import re

PHONE = re.compile(r"\d{3}-\d{3}-\d{4}")

def valid_phone(s):
    return PHONE.fullmatch(s) is not None

print(valid_phone("555-867-5309"))   # True
print(valid_phone("555-8675"))       # False
print(valid_phone("x 555-867-5309")) # False (extra chars)

steps.title: Clean and transform text with sub
steps.explanation: `re.sub` replaces every match. A replacement can reference captured groups with `\1` (or `\g<name>`), and passing a function as the replacement lets you compute each substitution.
steps.python:
import re

# collapse runs of whitespace into a single space
messy = "too    many\t\tspaces\n\nhere"
print(re.sub(r"\s+", " ", messy))

# reformat dates YYYY-MM-DD -> DD/MM/YYYY using group references
print(re.sub(r"(\d{4})-(\d{2})-(\d{2})", r"\3/\2/\1", "2026-06-29"))

# uppercase every word using a function replacement
print(re.sub(r"\b\w+\b", lambda m: m.group(0).upper(), "hello there"))

practice: Extend the log pattern to also capture an optional response time like ` 0.42s` at the end of the line using `(?P<time>[\d.]+)s`.
Write a regex that finds all hashtags (`#word`) in a string and returns them with `findall`.
Tighten the phone validator to also accept an optional `+1 ` country code prefix.
Use `re.sub` with a function to mask all but the last 4 digits of any credit-card-like number (`\d{13,16}`).
Add `re.IGNORECASE` to a word-search pattern and confirm it matches 'Python', 'python', and 'PYTHON' alike.
